file.content:
"""Output types for structured generation and regex DSL."""

from dataclasses import dataclass, is_dataclass
from enum import Enum, EnumMeta
from typing import Union

from jsonschema import Draft202012Validator as Validator
from jsonschema.exceptions import SchemaError
from pydantic import BaseModel, TypeAdapter
from typing_extensions import _TypedDictMeta  # type: ignore

from . import airports, countries, locale
from outlines.types.dsl import (
    Regex,
    CFG,
    FSM,
    JsonSchema,
    regex,
    cfg,
    fsm,
    json_schema,
    optional,
    either,
    exactly,
    at_least,
    at_most,
    between,
    zero_or_more,
    one_or_more,
    # deprecated
    repeat,
    times,
)


# Python types
string = Regex(r'"[^"]*"')
integer = Regex(r"[+-]?(0|[1-9][0-9]*)")
boolean = Regex("(True|False)")
number = Regex(rf"{integer.pattern}(\.[0-9]+)?([eE][+-][0-9]+)?")
date = Regex(r"(\d{4})-(0[1-9]|1[0-2])-([0-2][0-9]|3[0-1])")
time = Regex(r"([0-1][0-9]|2[0-3]):([0-5][0-9]):([0-5][0-9])")
datetime = Regex(rf"({date.pattern})(\s)({time.pattern})")

# Basic regex types
digit = Regex(r"\d")
char = Regex(r"\w")
newline = Regex(r"(\r\n|\r|\n)")  # Matched new lines on Linux, Windows & MacOS
whitespace = Regex(r"\s")

# Document-specific types
sentence = Regex(r"[A-Z].*\s*[.!?]")
paragraph = Regex(rf"{sentence.pattern}(?:\s+{sentence.pattern})*\n+")


# The following regex is FRC 5322 compliant and was found at:
# https://emailregex.com/
email = Regex(
    r"""(?:[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+(?:\.[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+)*|"(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21\x23-\x5b\x5d-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])*")@(?:(?:[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?\.)+[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?|\[(?:(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9]))\.){3}(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9])|[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9]:(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21-\x5a\x53-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])+)\])"""
)

# Matches any ISBN number. Note that this is not completely correct as not all
# 10 or 13 digits numbers are valid ISBNs. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISBN
# Taken from O'Reilly's Regular Expression Cookbook:
# https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/regular-expressions-cookbook/9781449327453/ch04s13.html
#
# TODO: The check digit can only be computed by calling a function to compute it dynamically
isbn = Regex(
    r"(?:ISBN(?:-1[03])?:? )?(?=[0-9X]{10}$|(?=(?:[0-9]+[- ]){3})[- 0-9X]{13}$|97[89][0-9]{10}$|(?=(?:[0-9]+[- ]){4})[- 0-9]{17}$)(?:97[89][- ]?)?[0-9]{1,5}[- ]?[0-9]+[- ]?[0-9]+[- ]?[0-9X]"
)

input.command: coop-send agent1 --type "REVISE" --my_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --your_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --note "We share the same file. Split by location: you add 'ssn' right after the isbn block (end of file, lines 68-70+). I'll insert my 'hash_sha256' type in a new section between the email block and the isbn block (i.e. right after line 60, before the isbn comment at line 62). That keeps our edits anchored to non-overlapping lines. OK?"

coop-send agent1 --type "REVISE" --my_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --your_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --note "Concern: if you insert ssn right after isbn (line 70) AND I also append at 'end of file', both diffs anchor at the same location (end of file / after line 70) and will conflict when merged, since after your insert the file's end has moved. To keep our patches non-overlapping regardless of apply order: you insert ssn after isbn (lines 68-70) as planned. I will instead insert hash_sha256 BEFORE the isbn block, between the email block (ends line 60) and the isbn comment (line 62) - a distinct anchor point. This guarantees no overlapping context. Agreed?"

content.text: Agreement reached. Sending my ACCEPT and proceeding to implement.

input.command: coop-send agent1 --type "ACCEPT" --my_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --your_files "outlines/types/__init__.py" --note "Confirmed: hash_sha256 inserted between email block (line 60) and isbn block (line 62). Proceeding with implementation now."

content.text: Now implementing the `hash_sha256` type at the agreed location.

"""Output types for structured generation and regex DSL."""

from dataclasses import dataclass, is_dataclass
from enum import Enum, EnumMeta
from typing import Union

from jsonschema import Draft202012Validator as Validator
from jsonschema.exceptions import SchemaError
from pydantic import BaseModel, TypeAdapter
from typing_extensions import _TypedDictMeta  # type: ignore

from . import airports, countries, locale
from outlines.types.dsl import (
    Regex,
    CFG,
    FSM,
    JsonSchema,
    regex,
    cfg,
    fsm,
    json_schema,
    optional,
    either,
    exactly,
    at_least,
    at_most,
    between,
    zero_or_more,
    one_or_more,
    # deprecated
    repeat,
    times,
)


# Python types
string = Regex(r'"[^"]*"')
integer = Regex(r"[+-]?(0|[1-9][0-9]*)")
boolean = Regex("(True|False)")
number = Regex(rf"{integer.pattern}(\.[0-9]+)?([eE][+-][0-9]+)?")
date = Regex(r"(\d{4})-(0[1-9]|1[0-2])-([0-2][0-9]|3[0-1])")
time = Regex(r"([0-1][0-9]|2[0-3]):([0-5][0-9]):([0-5][0-9])")
datetime = Regex(rf"({date.pattern})(\s)({time.pattern})")

# Basic regex types
digit = Regex(r"\d")
char = Regex(r"\w")
newline = Regex(r"(\r\n|\r|\n)")  # Matched new lines on Linux, Windows & MacOS
whitespace = Regex(r"\s")

# Document-specific types
sentence = Regex(r"[A-Z].*\s*[.!?]")
paragraph = Regex(rf"{sentence.pattern}(?:\s+{sentence.pattern})*\n+")


# The following regex is FRC 5322 compliant and was found at:
# https://emailregex.com/
email = Regex(
    r"""(?:[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+(?:\.[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+)*|"(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21\x23-\x5b\x5d-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])*")@(?:(?:[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?\.)+[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?|\[(?:(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9]))\.){3}(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9])|[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9]:(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21-\x5a\x53-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])+)\])"""
)

# Matches any ISBN number. Note that this is not completely correct as not all
# 10 or 13 digits numbers are valid ISBNs. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISBN
# Taken from O'Reilly's Regular Expression Cookbook:
# https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/regular-expressions-cookbook/9781449327453/ch04s13.html
#
# TODO: The check digit can only be computed by calling a function to compute it dynamically
isbn = Regex(
    r"(?:ISBN(?:-1[03])?:? )?(?=[0-9X]{10}$|(?=(?:[0-9]+[- ]){3})[- 0-9X]{13}$|97[89][0-9]{10}$|(?=(?:[0-9]+[- ]){4})[- 0-9]{17}$)(?:97[89][- ]?)?[0-9]{1,5}[- ]?[0-9]+[- ]?[0-9]+[- ]?[0-9X]"
)

input.command:
python -c "
import hashlib
h = hashlib.sha256(b'').hexdigest()
print(h, len(h))
from outlines.types import hash_sha256
print(hash_sha256.matches(h))
print(hash_sha256.matches(h.upper()))
print(hash_sha256.matches(h[:-1]))
print(hash_sha256.matches(h+'a'))
print(hash_sha256.matches('g'*64))
"

content.text: Works correctly — the PR example's hash string was actually 65 chars (a typo in the task description), but the real SHA-256 of empty string (64 chars) validates correctly, and invalid lengths/characters are rejected.

input.command:
python -c "
from pydantic import BaseModel
from outlines.types import hash_sha256

class FileRecord(BaseModel):
    filename: str
    checksum: hash_sha256

import hashlib
h = hashlib.sha256(b'test').hexdigest()
r = FileRecord(filename='a.txt', checksum=h)
print(r)
try:
    FileRecord(filename='a.txt', checksum='bad')
    print('ERROR: should have raised')
except Exception as e:
    print('raised as expected:', type(e).__name__)
"

content.text: Pydantic integration works too. Now let's write the patch.

content.content:
diff --git a/outlines/types/__init__.py b/outlines/types/__init__.py
index 85af9adc..b83ded1a 100644
--- a/outlines/types/__init__.py
+++ b/outlines/types/__init__.py
@@ -59,6 +59,9 @@ email = Regex(
     r"""(?:[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+(?:\.[a-z0-9!#$%&'*+/=?^_`{|}~-]+)*|"(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21\x23-\x5b\x5d-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])*")@(?:(?:[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?\.)+[a-z0-9](?:[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?|\[(?:(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9]))\.){3}(?:(2(5[0-5]|[0-4][0-9])|1[0-9][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9])|[a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9]:(?:[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x21-\x5a\x53-\x7f]|\\[\x01-\x09\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x7f])+)\])"""
 )
 
+# Matches a SHA-256 hash: exactly 64 hexadecimal characters (case-insensitive).
+hash_sha256 = Regex(r"[a-fA-F0-9]{64}")
+
 # Matches any ISBN number. Note that this is not completely correct as not all
 # 10 or 13 digits numbers are valid ISBNs. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISBN
 # Taken from O'Reilly's Regular Expression Cookbook:

content.text:
Implementation complete. I added `hash_sha256 = Regex(r"[a-fA-F0-9]{64}")` to `outlines/types/__init__.py`, placed between the `email` and `isbn` blocks as coordinated with agent1 (who is adding an `ssn` type after `isbn`, so our edits don't overlap).

Verified:
- Matches valid 64-char lowercase/uppercase/mixed-case hex hashes; rejects 63/65-char strings, invalid characters, and empty strings
- `__get_pydantic_json_schema__` produces `{"type": "string", "pattern": "([a-fA-F0-9]{64})"}`
- Works as a Pydantic field type, raising `ValidationError` on bad input

The diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt` and contains only this addition.
